perturbation.original_text: MMT and SIGA shall each, at their sole cost and expense, procure and maintain (a) commercial general liability insurance in amounts not less than $[***] per incident and $[***] annual aggregate, and (c) product liability insurance in amounts not less than $[***] annual aggregate, and each naming the other Party as additional insured. MMT and SIGA shall maintain such insurance throughout the Term, and shall from time to time provide copies of certificates of such insurance the other Party upon request.
perturbation.changed_text: MMT and SIGA shall each, at their sole cost and expense, procure and maintain commercial general liability insurance and product liability insurance. MMT and SIGA shall maintain such insurance throughout the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the minimum insurance coverage amounts and the requirement to provide certificates of insurance creates a legal risk. Without these specific requirements, the contract does not ensure adequate protection for potential liabilities, which can violate regulations concerning adequate financial responsibility for businesses.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Ins. Law § 2103 concerns licensing requirements for insurance agents.  While it doesn't directly specify minimum coverage, it is related to the broader regulatory framework concerning insurance.  By removing the specific insurance coverage requirements, the contract increases the risk that either party may not be adequately insured. This indirectly contradicts the intention of insurance regulations, which aim to ensure financial responsibility. Lack of specificity could also lead to disputes and potential legal challenges regarding the adequacy of insurance coverage, undermining the contract's enforceability, since it provides no definite means for either party to ascertain whether the other is properly insured. By being vague about the levels, it can violate regulations concerning adequate financial responsibility for businesses.

2025-03-28 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2103 Insurance agents; licensing Insurance (ISC) CHAPTER 28, ARTICLE 21 § 2103. Insurance agents; licensing. (a) The superintendent may issue a license to any person, firm or corporation who or which has complied with the requirements of this chapter, authorizing such licensee to act as an insurance agent with respect to the lines of authority for life insurance, variable life and variable annuity products, or accident and health insurance and sickness or any other line of authority deemed to be similar by the superintendent, including for this purpose, health maintenance organization contracts, legal services insurance or with respect to any combination of the above, as specified in such license, on behalf of any insurer, fraternal benefit society or health maintenance organization, which is authorized to do such kind or kinds of insurance or health maintenance organization business in this state. (b) The superintendent may issue a license to any person, firm, association or corporation who or which has complied with the requirements of this chapter, authorizing the licensee to act as agent of any authorized insurer, other than an insurer specified in subsection (b) of section two thousand one hundred fifteen of this article, with respect to the lines of authority for accident and health or sickness, property, casualty, personal lines or any other line of authority granted other than life, and variable life and variable annuity products, which such insurer is authorized to do in this state. (c) Any such license issued to a firm or association shall authorize only the members thereof, named in such license as sub-licensees, to act individually as agents thereunder, and any such license issued to a corporation shall authorize only the officers and directors thereof, named in such license as sub-licensees, to act individually as agents thereunder. Every sub-licensee, acting as insurance agent pursuant to such a license shall be authorized so to act only in the name of the
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies minimum insurance coverage amounts and requires providing certificates of insurance. The changed text removes these specific requirements. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this creates a legal risk because without these specifics, the contract doesn't ensure adequate protection and can violate financial responsibility regulations. The Law Explanation suggests this vagueness undermines the contract's enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (NY Ins. Law § 2103) relates to insurance agent licensing, which is part of the broader regulatory framework concerning insurance. The removal of specific coverage amounts weakens the assurance of adequate financial responsibility, thereby contradicting the intent of insurance regulations. This omission directly affects the enforceability and compliance of the insurance provision.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will conduct appropriate screening of employees, consultants, contractors or agents that perform services on behalf of such Party under this Agreement against the relevant Restricted Party Lists. Each Party will notify the other Party promptly, but in no event later than five (5) Business Days, upon becoming aware that any of its employees, consultants, contractors or agents has been excluded, debarred, suspended or is otherwise ineligible, or is the subject of exclusion, debarment or suspension proceedings by any Regulatory Authority.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will conduct screening of employees, consultants, contractors or agents that perform services on behalf of such Party under this Agreement. Each Party will notify the other Party upon becoming aware that any of its employees, consultants, contractors or agents has been excluded, debarred, suspended or is otherwise ineligible, or is the subject of exclusion, debarment or suspension proceedings by any Regulatory Authority.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'appropriate' is removed and the notification deadline ('promptly, but in no event later than five (5) Business Days') is deleted. This introduces legal risk by making the screening process vague and open to interpretation. The delay in notification also creates legal risk as it does not ensure immediate action, potentially violating anti-corruption laws and regulations regarding dealing with debarred individuals or entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: 31 CFR § 501.602 mandates that entities implement risk-based compliance programs, which require active screening and timely notification regarding dealings with sanctioned parties. The modified text weakens these requirements by removing 'appropriate' and deleting the strict 5-day deadline for notification. This can be interpreted as a failure to maintain an effective compliance program as it increases the possibility of dealing with restricted parties without proper due diligence or timely reporting, thereby violating the OFAC regulation.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'appropriate screening' and a notification deadline of 'no later than five (5) Business Days.' The changed text removes 'appropriate' and the deadline. The Law Explanation states that 31 CFR § 501.602 requires 'active screening and timely notification.' The Perturbation Explanation highlights that removing 'appropriate' makes screening vague, and deleting the deadline does not ensure immediate action, potentially violating anti-corruption laws. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state the necessity of specific deadlines, it refers to 31 CFR 501.602, which the Law Explanation ties to the need for timely notification. Scraped Law Snippet 2 lists statutes, rules, and regulations relating to OFAC settlements and civil penalties including 31 CFR 501, again tying back to the regulation. The changes weaken the compliance program by increasing the risk of dealing with restricted parties, which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: MMT will provide appropriate (as determined by MMT in its sole discretion) training (including regarding compliance with applicable Laws) of the MMT Promotion Personnel who will be communicating with potential customers about the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: MMT will provide training of the MMT Promotion Personnel who will be communicating with potential customers about the Product.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase '(as determined by MMT in its sole discretion) training (including regarding compliance with applicable Laws)' weakens the obligation to ensure comprehensive legal compliance training is provided. The lack of mandated legal compliance training creates a risk that the promotion personnel might inadvertently violate laws, leading to liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b prohibits kickbacks and other illegal remuneration in connection with federal healthcare programs. Adequate compliance training is vital to ensure that promotional activities do not violate these anti-kickback laws. Removing the phrase 'compliance with applicable Laws' implies the training does not necessarily cover these crucial legal aspects, increasing the risk that MMT's promotional activities could inadvertently violate these anti-kickback statutes. Moreover, by not mandating legal compliance training, the contract risks that activities would violate applicable Laws.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for training regarding compliance with applicable laws. While the original text specified 'appropriate training (including regarding compliance with applicable Laws),' the changed text only states 'training.' This omission creates a loophole where MMT could provide training without covering legally mandated compliance aspects, potentially violating laws like 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b, which addresses criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs. The original text ensured consideration of laws; removing it risks activities that could violate applicable Laws. The scraped snippet 1 states that section 1320a-7b covers "Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs", which further supports the justification that the changed text poses a legal issue.
